title: Wine Moms Switched to Weed, But Their Wounds Haven't Changed
gist: A psychoanalyst argues that mothers' shift from 'wine mom' coping to daily cannabis use is not a new problem but the same structural wound -- isolated, understaffed early motherhood -- passed down intergenerationally through attachment patterns, and proposes distinguishing substance use as self-treatment versus avoidance based on whether it increases or decreases a mother's capacity to be present with her child.

themes.theme: From wine mom to weed mom: same wound, new substance

insights.claim: In a recent Atlantic essay, Taylor Mitchem, who brought her baby home in March 2020 into pandemic isolation with no village and a husband too frightened to help, settled by age two-and-a-half of her child into a daily cannabis ritual she calls 'gardening' to cope with long toddler-care afternoons -- functionally replacing the prior generation's 'wine mom' rosé habit.
insights.speaker: Erica Komisar
insights.so_what: Frames a specific, dateable cohort (pandemic-era new mothers) as a case study for how isolation-driven coping substances evolve in form (alcohol to cannabis) without the underlying structural cause changing.
insights.quote: The substance has changed. The structure has not.
insights.locator: In a recent essay for The Atlantic

insights.claim: The author argues culture has sold an entire generation of mothers the same message: that the way through the difficulty of raising a young child is to feel less of it, via substances rather than support.
insights.speaker: Erica Komisar
insights.so_what: Identifies a repeatable cultural pattern (numbing rather than resourcing mothers) that predicts continued substance substitution trends regardless of which specific substance is in vogue.
insights.quote: We have sold an entire generation of mothers the same quiet proposition: that the way through the difficulty of raising a young child is to feel less of it.

insights.claim: Married mothers report significantly higher levels of happiness than unmarried women, per recent IFS research, indicating not all mothers are equally struggling with the isolation described in the piece.
insights.speaker: Erica Komisar
insights.so_what: Marital status is flagged as a moderating variable on maternal wellbeing -- relevant context for not over-generalizing the isolation narrative to all mothers.
insights.locator: It's important to note that not all mothers are struggling

themes.theme: The attachment mechanism behind maternal struggle

insights.claim: The author argues motherhood is not simply a stressor layered onto a pre-existing condition (e.g., anxiety); rather, the developmental work a mother does with her infant reaches down into her own infancy, so a woman securely attached as a baby tends to find early motherhood's distress more bearable, while a woman raised by parents 'present in body but absent in attention' finds her own unmet childhood needs reactivated by her child's needs.
insights.speaker: Erica Komisar
insights.so_what: Provides a causal mechanism (intergenerational attachment transmission) for why some mothers are disproportionately vulnerable to substance-based coping in early motherhood, useful for targeting clinical or policy interventions at attachment history rather than just present circumstances.
insights.locator: But there is something about that wound we tend to skip

insights.claim: The author connects the two generations directly: mothers who were 'present in body but absent in attention' due to lacking leave, a village, or a supportive partner produce daughters whose insecurity is described as the fingerprint of the conditions imposed on their own mothers -- society's long disinvestment in caregiving now travels down generations on its own.
insights.speaker: Erica Komisar
insights.so_what: Reframes individual maternal mental-health struggles as a multi-generational societal caregiving-underinvestment problem, arguing against purely individual-level clinical explanations.
insights.quote: We are not looking at a chain of fragile women. We are looking at a society that has disinvested in caregiving for so long that the wound now travels down the generations on its own.
insights.locator: Now hold those two truths together

insights.claim: The author states secure attachment is not a soft sentiment but biology: an infant's nervous system is literally built into relationship with an attuned adult, and this requires time, proximity, and a present adult who is not herself running on empty -- conditions a culture that makes all three scarce cannot simply will into being.
insights.speaker: Erica Komisar
insights.so_what: Positions attachment science as a hard constraint rather than an ideological preference, implying policy solutions (time, proximity, support) are non-negotiable inputs, not optional extras.
insights.quote: An infant's nervous system is literally built into relationships to an attuned adult.
insights.locator: I want to be clear, because this is the part both camps refuse to hear

insights.claim: The author proposes a practical test for distinguishing legitimate self-treatment from avoidance in mothers' substance use: treatment increases a mother's capacity to be present with her child, while avoidance decreases her need to be present -- the same substance can serve either function depending on what it is asked to do.
insights.speaker: Erica Komisar
insights.so_what: Offers a clinically actionable heuristic (capacity-increasing vs. need-decreasing) for evaluating maternal substance use case-by-case rather than moralizing about the substance itself.
insights.quote: Treatment increases your capacity to be present with your child. Avoidance decreases your need to be.
insights.locator: That brings me to the hardest question

insights.claim: A mother with diagnosable anxiety who was failed by a medical system that dismissed her postpartum symptoms, and who finds that cannabis quiets her enough to engage with her child, may be doing something closer to self-treatment than self-erasure.
insights.speaker: Erica Komisar
insights.so_what: Flags postpartum care system failures (dismissed symptoms) as an upstream driver pushing mothers toward self-medication with cannabis, a gap a better-functioning postpartum mental-health system could close.
insights.locator: In my practice, it is rarely obvious

insights.claim: The author's prescription is not stay-at-home versus working motherhood, but centering the child during the years of peak vulnerability through paid parental leave, flexible and humane work, fathers treating early caregiving as essential work, and a culture that treats caregiving as real work in policy, not just sentiment.
insights.speaker: Erica Komisar
insights.so_what: Lays out a concrete policy basket (paid leave, flexible work, paternal involvement, caregiving recognition) that maps directly onto family/labor policy debates and could be used as a scorecard for evaluating proposed legislation.
insights.locator: That means paid parental leave, flexible and humane work

insights.claim: The author frames women's ability to 'have it all' as seasonal rather than simultaneous -- there is a season for slowing down to be present with young children, and another for re-entering the world with renewed purpose once the child's inner world is secure.
insights.speaker: Erica Komisar
insights.so_what: A sequencing model (career and caregiving as alternating seasons, not concurrent demands) that pushes back on always-on dual-track expectations for working mothers.
insights.quote: Women can have it all, but, as I have written before, not all at once.
